report:
# Personalized Health Report: Gray Randolph

## Introduction

Testing to see if the editing will work

Gray, your health journey over the past 2.5 years has been challenging and complex. Since that acute illness in October 2022, you've been caught in a cycle of severe digestive distress, chronic congestion, anxiety, brain fog, and profound fatigue. Despite trying numerous treatments, from multiple antibiotics to herbal protocols, symptom relief has remained temporary at best. Your experience of complete, albeit brief, relief following a 40-minute breathwork session, and the temporary improvements from certain antibiotics, tells us something crucial: your body retains the capacity to heal when the right levers are engaged.

At Prism, we approach health through a bioenergetic lens. This means we view your symptoms not as isolated problems, but as interconnected manifestations of disrupted energy production at the cellular level. When your gut becomes compromised by chronic infection and dysbiosis, it doesn't just cause digestive symptoms. Bacterial endotoxins and inflammatory signals cross your intestinal barrier, burden your liver, activate your stress response systems, suppress your thyroid function, and drain your immune reserves. This cascade impairs mitochondrial energy production throughout your body, manifesting as the brain fog, fatigue, hormonal dysfunction, and anxiety you're experiencing.

Your case demonstrates this principle clearly. The acute infection in 2022 triggered persistent gut dysbiosis (confirmed with pathogens like Cryptosporidium, E. coli, and Staph). This created intestinal permeability, allowing bacterial toxins to enter your bloodstream continuously. Your liver, tasked with detoxifying these endotoxins, became overwhelmed, leading to reduced protein synthesis (reflected in your low albumin). The chronic inflammatory state elevated stress hormones and sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), sequestering your testosterone despite adequate production. Inflammatory cytokines suppressed your thyroid hormone conversion, leaving you with borderline low T3 despite normal TSH. Your immune system, constantly fighting infections it cannot fully clear, became exhausted (shown by your low white blood cells and lymphocytes). And dysbiosis created histamine overload, driving your chronic congestion, food sensitivities, and anxiety.

The good news is that this cascade is reversible. By addressing the root drivers—clearing persistent infections, healing your gut barrier, modulating inflammation and histamine, supporting your autonomic nervous system, and optimizing metabolic cofactors—we can break the cycle and restore your energy, mental clarity, hormonal balance, and quality of life.

## Assessment Findings

### Previous Diagnostics

Here is your previous lab work viewed through a new lens:

**Albumin**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| Albumin | Your Result: 4.2 g/dL (reference: 4.3-5.2) |  |

Low albumin reflects impaired liver synthesis likely from chronic gut inflammation and endotoxin burden. In the context of severe IBS and 2.5 years of illness, this indicates the gut-liver axis is compromised, reducing production of this critical protein needed for nutrient transport and maintaining oncotic pressure.

---

**Testosterone**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| Free Testosterone | Your Result: 6.5 pg/mL (reference: 9.3-26.5) | 100-240 pg/mL |
| Total Testosterone | Your Result: 622 ng/dL (reference: 264-916) | >75th percentile based on age |

This discrepancy is striking and clinically significant. Your total testosterone appears adequate, but your free (bioavailable) testosterone is severely low—well below even the conventional reference range and dramatically below Prism's optimal target. This pattern indicates elevated SHBG is sequestering testosterone, making it biologically unavailable. Chronic inflammation and endotoxemia drive SHBG production in the liver, directly contributing to your loss of pleasure, low libido, lost morning erections, fatigue, and impaired gut motility.

---

**Thyroid Function**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| TSH | Your Result: 1.34 mIU/L (reference: 0.40-4.50) | <1.5 uIU/mL |
| Free T4 | Your Result: 1.2 ng/dL (reference: 0.8-1.8) | 1.2-1.8 ng/dL |
| Free T3 | Your Result: 3.5 pg/mL (reference: 2.3-4.2) | 3.5-4.5 pg/mL |
| Reverse T3 | Your Result: 12 ng/dL (reference: 8-25) | 9.2-16.7 ng/dL |

While these values fall within conventional ranges, they tell a story of functional hypothyroidism. Your free T3 and free T4 sit at the absolute bottom of Prism's optimal ranges. T3 drives cellular metabolism, and levels this low contribute directly to your fatigue, brain fog, constipation, and sluggish gut motility. Chronic inflammation from gut dysbiosis suppresses the enzymes that convert T4 to T3, while stress hormones favor production of inactive reverse T3. Even with mid-range reverse T3, competition at cellular receptors may be blunting your metabolic drive.

---

**Immune Function**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| WBC | Your Result: 4.8 x10E3/uL (reference: 3.4-10.8) | 5.3-9.0 x10E3/uL |
| Absolute Lymphocytes | Your Result: 1.6 x10E3/uL (reference: 0.7-3.1) | 1612.5-3137.5 cells/uL |

Your white blood cell count and lymphocytes are below Prism's optimal ranges, indicating immune exhaustion. After 2.5 years of fighting chronic gut infections (Cryptosporidium, E. coli, Staph), your immune system is depleted. Low lymphocyte counts are associated with chronic infection, vitamin D deficiency, and hypothyroidism—all of which apply in your case. This depletion impairs your ability to clear pathogens, perpetuating the cycle of dysbiosis.

---

**Mineral Status**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| Zinc, RBC | Your Result: 11.9 mg/L (reference: 9.0-14.7) | Not specified in database |
| Copper, RBC | Your Result: 0.68 mg/L (reference: 0.53-0.91) | Not specified in database |

Your zinc and copper levels are mid-range, and the ratio appears reasonable. However, your advisor notes indicate high hair copper, suggesting tissue accumulation and mobilization of free copper contributing to oxidative stress. Zinc optimization is critical given its roles in gut barrier integrity, immune function, testosterone production, and thyroid conversion. Careful, strategic zinc supplementation can help displace excess copper while supporting healing.

---

**Other Markers**

| Test | Result | Prism Optimal Range |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| Hemoglobin | Your Result: 15.6 g/dL (reference: 13.0-17.7) | 13.2-17.1 g/dL |
| MCV | Your Result: 94 fL (reference: 79-97) | 85-95 fL |
| Creatinine | Your Result: 1.23 mg/dL (reference: 0.76-1.27) | Not specified in database |
| Glucose (fasting) | Your Result: 82 mg/dL (reference: 70-99) | 70-90 mg/dL |
| Candida Antibodies (IgG, IgM, IgA) | Your Result: Negative for all three | Not specified in database |

Your hemoglobin is healthy, ruling out anemia as a cause of fatigue. MCV at the upper end may suggest borderline B12 or folate status, consistent with impaired gut absorption. High-normal creatinine may reflect muscle breakdown from chronic stress and elevated cortisol. Fasting glucose is optimal, indicating preserved insulin sensitivity. Negative Candida antibodies suggest systemic Candida is not currently driving your symptoms, though localized gut fungal issues cannot be ruled out.

---

### Key Patterns

#### Chronic Gut Dysbiosis with Pathogenic Overgrowth

Your take-home assessment revealed Bristol type 6-7 stools with undigested food, indicating severe diarrhea and malabsorption. You rated constipation, bloating, distention, diarrhea, flatulence, and lower abdominal pain all at 5 out of 5 severity. Testing has confirmed pathogenic organisms: Cryptosporidium, E. coli, and Staph. Your advisor notes high fecal calprotectin, a marker of severe intestinal inflammation. The temporary but complete symptom relief you experienced with Levofloxacin and Azithromycin (both for approximately two weeks) confirms that bacterial and parasitic overgrowth is central to your presentation. The fact that herbal antimicrobial protocols consistently worsened your symptoms suggests either die-off reactions overwhelming your already burdened detoxification systems, or specific sensitivities to compounds in those formulas.

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Gut dysbiosis is the root node of your symptom cascade. Pathogenic bacteria produce endotoxin (lipopolysaccharide), which crosses your compromised intestinal barrier and triggers systemic inflammation. This endotoxin burden suppresses mitochondrial function throughout your body, activates your HPA axis (stress response), and drives the downstream consequences of impaired cellular energy production: fatigue, brain fog, immune dysfunction, and hormonal suppression.

#### Severe Systemic Inflammation and Histamine Intolerance

You rated chronic congestion and mucus buildup both at 5 out of 5 severity, with symptoms worsening after eating. Your questionnaire reveals severe reactions to high-histamine and histamine-liberating foods (frozen fruit, fermented foods like sauerkraut, cheese, alcohol, and even supplements like PHGG that triggered a "massive histamine response"). Your somewhat foamy urine suggests inflammatory metabolites and bile salts being excreted. Low albumin further confirms chronic hepatic inflammation from endotoxin processing.

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Dysbiosis produces excess histamine in the gut while simultaneously impairing production of diamine oxidase (DAO), the enzyme that degrades histamine. This creates systemic histamine overload. Additionally, gut inflammation and endotoxemia trigger mast cell degranulation throughout the body, releasing more histamine and inflammatory mediators. This drives your respiratory symptoms, anxiety, food sensitivities, and amplifies gut pain perception through heightened sensory nerve activation.

#### Stress Hormone Dysregulation and Autonomic Imbalance

You rated waking up during sleep at 5 out of 5, specifically later in the night to urinate (5-6 times per day urination frequency). Non-restful sleep rated 3 out of 5, nightmares 2 out of 5. You feel hyper-aware, jittery, and restless (4 out of 5), with heart pounding sensations (2 out of 5). Irritability rated 3 out of 5. Remarkably, you experienced complete symptom relief for an entire day after a 40-minute breathwork session, demonstrating that vagal nerve activation and parasympathetic engagement can override your symptom cascade when sufficiently stimulated.

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Chronic gut irritation activates sympathetic "fight or flight" responses via the gut-brain axis. Endotoxin and inflammatory cytokines signal the hypothalamus to increase cortisol and adrenaline production. This sympathetic overdrive disrupts sleep architecture, increases urination (via effects on antidiuretic hormone), creates anxiety and hypervigilance, and paradoxically worsens gut motility and immune function. The cycle perpetuates: stress worsens gut function, gut dysfunction increases stress signaling. Your breathwork response proves your nervous system retains plasticity and can be retrained.

#### Metabolic and Thyroid Dysfunction

You rated brain fog and low energy at 4 out of 5, with afternoon crashes after lunch at 3 out of 5. Your labs show free T3 at 3.5 pg/mL (bottom of optimal range) and free T4 at 1.2 ng/dL (bottom of optimal range). Your take-home assessment showed resting heart rate averaging 83 bpm, slightly elevated, which can paradoxically occur in functional hypothyroidism when stress hormones compensate for low thyroid drive. Low WBC (4.8) and lymphocytes (1.6) indicate immune metabolic exhaustion.

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Inflammatory cytokines from chronic gut inflammation suppress deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to active T3. Low T3 means reduced mitochondrial ATP production in every cell, manifesting as fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and impaired gut motility (worsening dysbiosis). Immune cells are highly metabolically active; without sufficient thyroid hormone and cellular energy, immune function declines, perpetuating chronic infections. This creates a vicious cycle where infection drives inflammation, inflammation suppresses thyroid function, low thyroid impairs immunity, and poor immunity allows infections to persist.

#### Hormonal Imbalances

You rated loss of pleasure at 5 out of 5, lack of sexual interest at 4 out of 5, and lost morning erections at 2 out of 5. Your labs reveal the mechanism: free testosterone at 6.5 pg/mL (versus Prism optimal 100-240 pg/mL), despite total testosterone at 622 ng/dL. This indicates elevated SHBG is binding and sequestering your testosterone. Your advisor notes DHEA-S was also low-normal.

**Bioenergetic Implication:** Chronic inflammation and endotoxin exposure increase hepatic SHBG production, sequestering testosterone and preventing it from activating androgen receptors. Additionally, chronic HPA axis activation and elevated cortisol directly suppress gonadal testosterone synthesis. Low androgens further impair gut motility, reduce motivation and hedonic drive (loss of pleasure/anhedonia), decrease muscle mass and metabolic rate, and worsen mood and anxiety. This hormonal suppression is both a consequence of impaired energy metabolism and a contributor to its perpetuation.

---

## Recommendations

### Precision Diagnostics

Objective diagnostic information is invaluable for tracking your progress and refining interventions. While symptoms guide treatment, biomarkers provide confirmation and early indication of improvement. The following tests are recommended:

**Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy, Total, Immunoassay**

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy, Total, Immunoassay | Ulta Lab Tests | $24.12 |

Given your low WBC and lymphocyte counts, severe IBS, and chronic congestion, assessing vitamin D status is essential. Vitamin D is foundational for immune regulation, gut barrier integrity, and HPA axis modulation. Deficiency is strongly associated with suppressed immune cell counts, chronic inflammation, and gut dysfunction. Optimizing vitamin D can improve immune surveillance against infections, reduce inflammatory cytokine production, and support intestinal tight junction integrity, directly addressing core drivers of your symptoms.

---

**Free T3, Free T4, Reverse T3, and TSH Panel**

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Free T3, Free T4, RT3 and TSH Panel | Ulta Lab Tests | $68.74 |

This panel provides comprehensive assessment of thyroid hormone production, activation, and potential conversion dysfunction. Your previous labs showed borderline low free T3 and T4. Measuring reverse T3 alongside active T3 evaluates whether chronic stress and inflammation are shunting T4 conversion toward the inactive reverse T3 pathway instead of active T3. This pattern is common in chronic illness and directly contributes to fatigue, brain fog, constipation, and gut dysmotility. Understanding your current thyroid status will guide whether thyroid hormone supplementation is warranted as you heal.

---

**Thyroid Peroxidase and Thyroglobulin Antibodies**

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Thyroid Peroxidase and Thyroglobulin Antibodies | Ulta Lab Tests | $33.45 |

These antibodies screen for autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's), which commonly presents with fatigue, borderline thyroid markers, and is often triggered by chronic gut dysfunction and intestinal permeability. If antibodies are elevated, it confirms autoimmune involvement and emphasizes the importance of gut healing to prevent further thyroid tissue destruction. If negative, it rules out this mechanism, clarifying that your thyroid dysfunction is purely functional and reversible with metabolic support.

---

**Plasma Zinc**

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Plasma Zinc | Ulta Lab Tests | $22.95 |

Zinc is a crucial cofactor for deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to T3. Your RBC zinc was mid-range, but functional zinc status assessed via plasma levels will clarify whether optimization is needed for thyroid conversion. Zinc is also essential for gut barrier tight junctions, immune T cell function, and testosterone production. Given your borderline low T3, gut inflammation, and low free testosterone, ensuring adequate zinc status is critical for supporting multiple healing pathways simultaneously.

---

**Copper**

| Diagnostic | Provider | Price |
|------------|----------|-------|
| Copper | (Provider not specified) | $45.95 |

Your advisor noted high hair copper levels, suggesting tissue accumulation and mobilization. Elevated free copper contributes to oxidative stress and inflammation, potentially hindering thyroid function and metabolic health. Assessing serum copper alongside zinc allows calculation of the copper-to-zinc ratio, providing insight into whether zinc supplementation protocols are effectively balancing mineral status or if copper is contributing to ongoing oxidative burden.

---

### Diet & Lifestyle Recommendations

#### Pro-Metabolic Dietary Principles

Adopting a pro-metabolic approach is foundational for simultaneously addressing your low energy, thyroid dysfunction, gut inflammation, and hormonal imbalances. This framework prioritizes nutrients and macronutrient ratios that optimize cellular energy production while avoiding foods that trigger fermentation in your dysbiotic gut.

The core principles are adequate protein, easily digestible carbohydrates, and emphasis on saturated fats while limiting polyunsaturated fats. Given your severe food sensitivities to high-fiber foods, fermented foods, and complex carbohydrates, focusing on simple, easily digestible sources is essential.

**Protein sources:** Prioritize various cuts of beef, lamb, bison, venison, eggs, shellfish, white fish, and if tolerated, cottage cheese and Greek yogurt. These provide essential amino acids for tissue repair, thyroid hormone production, and neurotransmitter synthesis without triggering gut fermentation.

**Carbohydrate sources:** Focus on simple, low-residue options that provide glucose for thyroid function and metabolic rate without feeding bacterial overgrowth. Excellent choices include fruit juices (especially orange juice), honey, cane sugar, very well-cooked white rice and potatoes, and fruits like grapes, apples, pineapple, and oranges. Avoid high-fiber and fermentable carbohydrates that exacerbate your symptoms.

**Fats:** Emphasize saturated fats from butter, ghee, coconut oil, beef tallow, and moderate amounts of extra virgin olive oil. Strictly limit polyunsaturated fats (soybean, canola, corn, cottonseed, sunflower, safflower oils) which are highly prevalent in packaged foods and restaurants. These oils produce inflammatory metabolites that suppress metabolic rate, promote hypothyroidism, and worsen gut inflammation. Given that commercial pork, chicken, and fish accumulate these fats, opt for leaner cuts of these meats, while ruminant red meat is naturally lower in polyunsaturated fats.

**Calcium:** Ensure adequate calcium from dairy products if tolerated (milk, cheese, Greek yogurt), canned fish with bones (sardines), or well-cooked greens. If these aren't well tolerated, consider calcium carbonate supplementation (1250 mg up to twice daily).

**Implementation:** Listen to your body carefully. This is not a restrictive protocol but a framework to construct meals that support your metabolism without triggering symptoms. As your gut heals, gradually expand your food variety. Regular meal timing supports circadian rhythms and gut motility. Never force eating when appetite is low, but avoid chronic undereating which activates stress responses.

---

#### Gelatin and Collagen for Gut Barrier Repair

Given your severe gut inflammation, intestinal barrier dysfunction, stomach pain, and acid reflux, incorporating gelatin and collagen is essential. These provide glycine, proline, and hydroxyproline—amino acids specifically needed for rebuilding intestinal mucosa and strengthening tight junctions in your compromised gut lining.

Start with 20-40g of gelatin or collagen daily. Collagen powder dissolves easily in liquids; gelatin can be mixed into foods like rice and ground beef. High-collagen foods include bone broth, chewy cuts of meat (chuck roast, ribs), connective tissue (shanks, oxtail), and whole shellfish (shrimp, scallops, oysters). These amino acids provide soothing, highly digestible building blocks for structural repair of your gut barrier.

---

#### Aloe Vera Juice

Aloe vera juice provides direct soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on the gastrointestinal mucosa. Use once daily (typically 2-4 ounces) to support gut mucosal healing and reduce inflammation. Choose pure aloe vera juice without added sugars or preservatives. This complements the structural support from gelatin/collagen by providing anti-inflammatory phytonutrients that calm irritated tissue.

---

#### Diaphragmatic Breathing and Box Breathing

Your complete, one-day symptom relief following a 40-minute breathwork session demonstrated the power of vagal nerve activation and parasympathetic engagement. Controlled breathing practices are among the most potent, cost-free interventions for modulating your autonomic nervous system, reducing sympathetic overdrive, and calming the gut-brain axis.

Diaphragmatic (belly) breathing involves slow, deep nasal breathing that engages the diaphragm fully. This technique increases carbon dioxide levels in the body, which has direct metabolic benefits including raising metabolic rate and producing potent anxiolytic (anti-anxiety) effects by enhancing parasympathetic tone.

**Box breathing** is a specific structured practice: inhale through the left nostril for 4 counts, hold for 4 counts, exhale through the left nostril for 4 counts, hold for 4 counts, and repeat. The left nostril preferentially activates parasympathetic pathways. Practice this for just 5 minutes anytime you experience gut distress, anxiety, or before bed. The brevity makes it sustainable multiple times daily.

Consider resuming 40-minute breathwork sessions (such as the one that gave you complete relief) periodically, as these create more profound nervous system resets. Your advisor shared a resource for box breathing: [5-Minute Box Breathing Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uQgE2KkKqAE)

---

#### Humming (Bhramari Pranayama)

Your chronic congestion and nasal inflammation persisted despite surgical intervention, suggesting the issue is not purely structural but involves vagal tone dysfunction and inflammatory processes. Humming, particularly the yogic practice of Bhramari Pranayama, offers a unique solution.

Humming creates vibrations that directly stimulate the vagus nerve, activating parasympathetic responses that lower stress hormones, improve heart rate variability, and reduce inflammatory signaling. Remarkably, humming increases nasal nitric oxide production up to 15-fold. Nitric oxide has vasodilatory effects (improving blood flow), anti-inflammatory properties, and antimicrobial activity—all beneficial for your chronic nasal inflammation.

Practice humming for several minutes daily. The technique involves taking a deep breath and producing a steady humming sound on the exhale, allowing the vibrations to resonate in your sinuses and skull. This simple practice can address your congestion through neurological and biochemical pathways that surgery cannot reach. Resource: [Learn How to Hum for Stress Resilience](insert link if available)

---

#### Acupressure Points

Acupressure provides targeted, self-administered nervous system modulation and digestive support through stimulation of specific mechanoreceptor and nerve pathways.

**PC6 (Pericardium 6 - Inner Gate):** Located on the inner forearm, about three finger-widths below the wrist crease, between the two central tendons. Stimulating PC6 enhances parasympathetic tone, reduces anxiety and sympathetic activation, and relieves nausea and gastrointestinal distress. This point is excellent for addressing your racing mind, jitteriness, and gut pain. Apply firm, sustained pressure for 1-3 minutes, or use circular massage. Resource: [PC6 Location and Stimulation Guide](insert link if available)

**ST-36 (Stomach 36 - Leg Three Miles):** Located about four finger-widths below the kneecap, one finger-width lateral to the tibia (shin bone). ST-36 is traditionally known for boosting overall vitality and digestive strength. Stimulation improves gastric motility, reduces inflammation, and supports the body's energy production for better digestive function. This complements PC6 by directly enhancing gut motility and reducing bloating. Apply firm pressure for 1-3 minutes per leg. Resource: [ST-36 Location and Stimulation Guide](insert link if available)

These points can be stimulated multiple times throughout the day, especially during symptom flares or before meals.

---

### Supplement Recommendations

**Acerola Cherry Extract (Vitamin C)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Acerola Cherry Extract (25% Vitamin C) Powder | [PureBulk](https://purebulk.com/products/acerola-cherry?_pos=10&_sid=3ba8b8694&_ss=r), use code "PRISM" at checkout for 10% off |

**Dosage and Timing:** 1.8g (approximately 1/2 teaspoon) daily, providing roughly 450mg of vitamin C

**Notes:** Vitamin C is rapidly depleted during chronic stress and HPA axis activation. Supplementation at 1000mg daily has been shown to reduce cortisol levels by approximately 35%, supporting adrenal function and stress resilience. Vitamin C is also a critical cofactor for collagen synthesis, essential for repairing your intestinal barrier. Additionally, vitamin C supports histamine degradation, directly addressing your severe histamine intolerance and associated symptoms like congestion and anxiety. Acerola cherry provides natural, food-based vitamin C with supporting bioflavonoids that enhance absorption and efficacy.

---

**Zinc Carnosine**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Zinc Carnosine | [Pure Encapsulations](https://www.pureformulas.com/product/peptic-care-zinc-l-carnosine-by-pure-encapsulations/1000005349?skuId=PE1728&gad_source=1) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 1 capsule (15mg) per day with your biggest meal

**Notes:** Zinc carnosine is uniquely effective for gastric and intestinal mucosal healing. The carnosine component stabilizes gut cells and accelerates healing of ulceration and inflammation, while zinc supports tight junction integrity and reduces intestinal permeability. Zinc is also essential for immune function (supporting your depleted T cells), testosterone production (addressing your low free testosterone), and inhibits aromatase enzyme that converts testosterone to estrogen. This formulation is optimized for therapeutic gut healing while providing systemic zinc benefits. The dosing is carefully calibrated given your high hair copper levels to provide benefit without excessive copper displacement in a short time frame.

---

**Liquid Zinc Picolinate**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Zinc Picolinate Powder | [LifeGivingStore](https://lifegivingstore.com/zinc-picolinate-powder/), use code "Optimize" at checkout for 10% off |

**Dosage and Timing:** 7.5mg three times daily with meals (22.5mg total per day)

**Notes:** This highly absorbable picolinate form provides additional systemic zinc support beyond the gut-specific zinc carnosine. Zinc is critical for thyroid hormone conversion, immune function, gut barrier repair, and testosterone production. Importantly, zinc supplementation increases metallothionein proteins that bind and help detoxify excess copper. Given your high hair copper burden indicating tissue mobilization and potential free copper contributing to oxidative stress, strategic zinc supplementation helps safely displace copper while maximizing therapeutic benefits for your gut, immune system, and hormones. The liquid form allows precise dosing spread across three meals for optimal absorption and utilization.

---

**Anderson Trace Minerals Complex**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Trace Minerals Complex | [Anderson](https://andersonhealthsolutions.com/products/trace-mineral-complex-full-spectrum-minerals-ionic-liquid-mineral-drops-for-water-electrolytes-hydration-supplement-2-fl-oz-glass-bottle) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 0.5 mL twice daily with your two biggest meals (1 mL total per day)

**Notes:** This liquid mineral complex provides crucial micronutrients, especially selenium and additional zinc, essential for thyroid function. Selenium is vital for deiodinase enzymes that convert inactive T4 to active T3 hormone. Your borderline low free T3 strongly suggests inadequate conversion, making selenium supplementation critical for optimizing thyroid function and boosting metabolism. The minerals also support antioxidant capacity (via selenoproteins), helping mitigate oxidative stress from chronic inflammation. The dose is reduced to half the standard recommendation because the formula contains copper, and given your high hair copper levels, you want to receive necessary cofactors while carefully managing total trace mineral intake to avoid further copper accumulation.

---

**Energin (Liquid B Vitamin Complex)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Energin | [Idealabsdc](https://idealabs.ecwid.com/Energin-p70230365) |

**Dosage and Timing:** Start with 6 drops orally in the morning daily. If no effect is noticed after 3-4 days, increase to 12 drops daily, and then if still no effect, 25 drops daily.

**Notes:** B vitamins are essential cofactors for mitochondrial energy production, particularly in the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain. They support neurotransmitter synthesis (dopamine, serotonin conversion to melatonin, GABA), proper red blood cell formation (addressing your high MCV which suggests B vitamin deficiency or poor utilization), and nervous system function including stress resilience. Given your severe brain fog, fatigue, and impaired gut absorption, this potent liquid formulation ensures superior bioavailability by bypassing digestive limitations. The sublingual delivery allows direct absorption into the bloodstream. Energin is specifically formulated for metabolic support with precise dosing guidance, making it ideal for your needs.

---

**Forefront Health Vitamin A, D, K (Topical)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Fat Soluble Vitamin Mix (A, D, K) | [Forefront Health](https://shop.forefronthealth.com/products/vitamin-adk-thyroid-supplement) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 2 servings applied topically to the skin per day. Apply to regions with thin skin (inner wrists and forearms are optimal) for best absorption.

**Notes:** Given your impaired gut absorption from chronic dysbiosis, topical delivery of fat-soluble vitamins ensures adequate intake by bypassing the digestive tract. Vitamin D is crucial for immune function (supporting your low WBC and lymphocytes), reducing inflammation, and modulating autoimmune responses. Vitamin A supports gut barrier integrity, immune regulation, and has pro-androgenic effects. Vitamin K2 directs calcium to proper tissues (bones and teeth) and away from soft tissues, reducing vascular calcification. Both vitamins A and D support testosterone production and thyroid function, directly addressing your hormonal deficiencies. Topical application ensures consistent delivery regardless of digestive function fluctuations.

---

**Human Milk Oligosaccharides (HMOs)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Human Milk Oligosaccharides | [Layer Origin](https://www.amazon.com/Layer-Origin-Oligosaccharide-Bioidentical-2-Fucosyllactose/dp/B08J8DTMGK) |

**Dosage and Timing:** One serving daily

**Notes:** After multiple rounds of antibiotics and recent antiparasitic treatment (Nitazoxanide), reestablishing a healthy bacterial ecosystem is essential. HMOs are specialized prebiotic compounds that selectively feed beneficial Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species while inhibiting pathogenic bacteria. Critically, HMOs do not feed pathogenic organisms or worsen dysbiosis, unlike many fiber-based prebiotics that exacerbated your symptoms (like PHGG). HMOs support gut barrier function, modulate immune responses, and help restore microbial diversity necessary for long-term gut health. This is a non-herbal, well-tolerated approach to colonization support that aligns with your constraint of histamine sensitivity and adverse reactions to herbal protocols. Continue taking alongside your Ultimate GI Repair product.

---

**Mastic Gum**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Mastic Gum | [Greco Gum](https://grecogum.com/#products), use code "OPTIMIZE" at checkout for free shipping |

**Notes:** Your symptoms worsening immediately after eating suggest stomach-level issues, and your advisor suspects Helicobacter pylori infection, a common driver of upper GI symptoms, stomach pain, and inflammation. Mastic gum has specific, well-documented antimicrobial activity against H. pylori through disruption of bacterial adhesion to gastric mucosa and direct bactericidal effects. It also provides gastroprotective benefits by supporting gastric mucosal healing and reducing inflammation independent of acid suppression.

Place a few drops of the liquid mastic in your mouth at once, waiting for them to soften, then chew to form one cohesive piece of gum. This can be chewed for multiple hours and re-chewed after removing if desired. There are no known side effects or risks, so chew as much as you want or benefit from, but aim for at least 30-45 minutes at a time for therapeutic effect. Many people chew mastic gum throughout the day. This addresses suspected H. pylori while supporting overall gastric health.

---

**MegaSporeBiotic (Spore-Based Probiotic)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| MegaSporeBiotic | [Microbiome Labs](https://www.amazon.com/Microbiome-Labs-MegaSporeBiotic/dp/B07TJ4TH8Q?m=A25W1DZX5FW5M7&marketplaceID=ATVPDKIKX0DER) |

**Dosage and Timing:** The recommended dose is one capsule daily, administered orally.

**Notes:** Spore-forming probiotics like MegaSporeBiotic exhibit strong antimicrobial actions against pathogenic bacteria, making them ideal for addressing suspected H. pylori and general gut dysbiosis from your confirmed pathogenic overgrowth (E. coli, Staph). Unlike many Lactobacillus strains that can be histamine-producing and may exacerbate your severe histamine intolerance, spore-based probiotics are generally well-tolerated and do not contribute to histamine burden. The spore forms survive stomach acid and colonize effectively, supporting long-term gut barrier integrity, immune modulation, and microbial balance. This complements the targeted anti-H. pylori effect of mastic gum with broader antimicrobial and colonization support.

---

**Histamine Digest (DAO Enzyme)**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Histamine Digest | [Ruscio Institute](https://store.drruscio.com/products/histamine-digest?variant=42174647435461) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 1 capsule in the morning, and then anytime histamine symptoms arise (typically before meals known to contain higher histamine)

**Notes:** This is essential given your severe histamine reactions documented throughout your questionnaire—chronic congestion and mucus (both rated 5/5), massive histamine response to PHGG, reactions to high-histamine foods like cheese, fermented foods, and alcohol. Histamine Digest contains diamine oxidase (DAO), the enzyme responsible for breaking down dietary histamine in the intestinal lumen before it can be absorbed into your bloodstream. Your gut inflammation and dysbiosis have impaired your endogenous DAO production, creating systemic histamine overload that drives your congestion, anxiety, food sensitivities, and GI distress. Supplemental DAO provides immediate functional support, allowing you to consume a wider variety of foods while your gut heals and native DAO production recovers. Take proactively before meals containing potential histamine triggers.

**Activated Charcoal**

| Supplement | Provider |
|------------|----------|
| Activated Charcoal | [Bulletproof](https://www.amazon.com/Bulletproof-Charcoal-Supports-Digestion-Capsules/dp/B06XYLNB4S) |

**Dosage and Timing:** 500mg once daily, taken at least 2 hours away from food and other supplements (charcoal binds minerals and medications, preventing absorption). Use as needed during periods of intense symptoms. Your advisor recommends potentially taking for 3 nights in a row NOW to help mop up die-off from your recent antiparasitic (Nitazoxanide) treatment.

**Notes:** Activated charcoal binds endotoxins (bacterial lipopolysaccharide), bacterial metabolites, and die-off byproducts in the GI tract, preventing their absorption and reducing toxic burden on your liver and systemic circulation. Given your recent antiparasitic treatment with Nitazoxanide, there may be significant die-off occurring, contributing to symptom flares. Charcoal helps "mop up" these toxins, reducing the inflammatory load that contributes to your bloating, brain fog, and systemic discomfort. Use during acute flares to accelerate recovery. Important: Charcoal can contribute to constipation in excess, so if this occurs, discontinue or lower the dosage. Always take separated from other supplements and medications to avoid binding and inactivating them.

**Additional Notes:**

Continue taking your **Ultimate GI Repair** product as you have been. The combination of that product with the newly added HMOs, probiotics, and gut barrier supports (zinc carnosine, gelatin/collagen, vitamin C) creates a comprehensive gut restoration protocol.

If your symptoms do not continue to improve over the next 4-6 weeks, or if they worsen, your advisor has noted that **minocycline** may be considered as an additional antimicrobial option. Minocycline is a tetracycline antibiotic with unique anti-inflammatory properties and good tissue penetration. This would be used strategically if it becomes clear that the recent antiparasitic did not fully resolve persistent infections. Contact your Prism advisor to discuss this option if needed.

## Conclusion

Gray, your case powerfully illustrates the bioenergetic principle that seemingly disparate symptoms—from gut pain to brain fog to low testosterone to chronic congestion—can all stem from a single root disruption: chronic gut dysbiosis driving systemic inflammation and impaired cellular energy production.

The cascade is clear: pathogenic overgrowth (Cryptosporidium, E. coli, Staph) created intestinal permeability, allowing endotoxin into your bloodstream. This endotoxin burden overwhelmed your liver (low albumin), activated chronic stress responses (sleep fragmentation, anxiety, frequent urination), suppressed thyroid conversion (low T3), elevated SHBG (sequestering testosterone), depleted immune reserves (low WBC and lymphocytes), and created histamine overload (congestion, food sensitivities). Each downstream effect further impaired your ability to produce cellular energy, creating a self-perpetuating cycle.

The good news is your body has already shown you it can heal. The complete symptom relief from breathwork proved your nervous system can reset when given the right signal. The temporary but profound improvements from certain antibiotics confirmed that clearing infections resolves your symptoms. The positive response to your recent antiparasitic suggests you may have finally addressed the Cryptosporidium that has been a core driver.

Now, the comprehensive protocol we've outlined addresses every layer of the cascade simultaneously: clearing remaining pathogens (mastic gum, MegaSporeBiotic), healing gut barrier integrity (zinc carnosine, gelatin/collagen, vitamin C, aloe vera), recolonizing beneficial bacteria (HMOs, spore probiotics), managing histamine (DAO enzyme), supporting thyroid and hormones (selenium, zinc, B vitamins, vitamins A/D/K), reducing stress and rebalancing autonomic tone (breathing practices, humming, acupressure), and providing metabolic fuel (pro-metabolic diet).

This is not about suppressing symptoms. It's about removing the barriers to your body's innate healing capacity and providing the raw materials and signals it needs to restore energy production, immune function, hormonal balance, and gut integrity.

Track your symptoms weekly. Revisit diagnostics in 8-12 weeks to objectively confirm progress (vitamin D, thyroid panel with antibodies, zinc, copper). Stay in close contact with your Prism advisor—adjustments to protocols based on your response are part of the process. If symptoms persist or worsen despite this comprehensive approach, additional antimicrobial intervention (minocycline) may be warranted.

You've been fighting this for 2.5 years with incredible persistence. You now have a clear map and a team supporting you. Trust the process, stay consistent, and know that healing is not only possible—it's already beginning.
